title: Chapter 13: Extraction
content: We ran. Not the measured, careful movement of our first Floor 8 expedition—this was a full-bore sprint, Luna pulling Jessica with one hand and a fire axe she'd grabbed from the hallway wall with the other, me bringing up the rear with nothing but adrenaline and desperately hoping my knees wouldn't give out.
Floor 8 to Floor 9: clear. The distraction had pulled the zombies down, and Derek's people were still on Floor 7 searching for a phantom device.

Floor 9: a zombie in the stairwell, a former maintenance worker still wearing his coveralls, standing in the corner like a forgotten coat rack. Luna didn't slow down. She swung the axe in a single, economy-of-motion arc that separated the zombie's head from its shoulders with a wet *thock* that I'd be hearing in my nightmares for weeks. She kept running.
Floor 10: empty.
Floor 11: I heard voices below us. Derek's, louder now, directing his people back up. He'd figured it out—or his paranoia had, which amounted to the same thing.
"The stairs!" a woman's voice shouted—Maria, presumably. "Someone's on the stairs!"
"They're heading up," Derek snarled. "Cut them off at 12. Go around—use the fire stairs on the east side."

Fire stairs. I'd accounted for the main stairwell but not the fire escape on the building's east face—a separate stair system that terminated at the roof. If Derek's people took that route, they'd reach Floor 12 before us.
"How far?" I gasped at Luna.
"Close. Two floors."
"Can we make it before—"
"Yes. Move."
I moved. My lungs burned, my legs screamed, and my heart was doing something medically inadvisable, but I moved because Luna said yes and Luna didn't lie.

Floor 12 landing. The barricade loomed ahead—a jumble of furniture and debris that would slow a normal person for precious seconds. Luna didn't even break stride. She handed Jessica to me—literally *pressed* the younger woman into my arms—and launched herself at the barricade, pushing, pulling, and throwing obstacles aside with terrifying efficiency. A couch flew. A bookshelf crumbled. The path cleared in five seconds.
"Go go go!"

I half-carried Jessica through the gap, her thin body light against my chest—she weighed almost nothing, light from too little food and too much fear. Luna followed, resealing the barricade behind us with speed that suggested she'd memorized every piece's position.
"Sarah!" I shouted. "Open the door!"

The titanium door of 12B swung open. Sarah stood in the doorway, her face pale but her hands steady, holding the camping stove's fuel canister like a weapon—less than useless against an armed attacker, but she'd been ready to try.
We tumbled inside. The door sealed behind us with its reassuring *thunk*, biometric lock engaging, titanium walls suddenly the only thing between us and a very angry man with very few scruples.

Jessica collapsed on the floor, sobbing—ugly, grateful, wailing sobs that shook her whole body. I'd seen that kind of crying before, in the mirror, the night my mom didn't call on my birthday. The sound of someone who'd held it together for too long and finally couldn't.
Luna crouched beside her, making soft hushing sounds—not words, just the low, humming tone she made when she was trying to be comforting. The same sound she'd made in the hallway on Day One.
Sarah knelt too, reaching out to touch Jessica's shoulder, then stopping—checking, asking permission without words. Jessica nodded, and Sarah's hand landed gently, carefully, on her back.
"It's okay," Sarah said. "You're safe now. No one's going to hurt you."
"I can't—he'll come for me—he said—" Jessica's words were lost in sobs, but I caught enough. Derek had made promises. Threats. The same playbook, another victim.
"He won't touch you," I said. "Not in here."

**QUEST UPDATE: JESSICA PARK — NON-ROMANCE TARGET**
**STATUS: Rescued (No quest attached)**
**NOTE: System does not reward non-romance rescues. This action was voluntary. Hollow Heart Protocol notes: Compassion validated without external incentive. Character assessment: POSITIVE.**
I read the notification twice. The system had noticed that I'd saved someone for no reward, and it had approved. Which was... nice? Creepy? Both?
"Your watch is glowing," Sarah said.
"It does that. Don't worry about it."
She gave me a look that said *I will absolutely worry about it, but not right now*, and turned back to Jessica.
The pounding on the stairwell door started ten minutes later.

Derek had made it to Floor 12, but not before we'd sealed the barricade, and the furniture pile was too heavy for him to move alone. He stood on the other side, hammering on the door, his voice fluctuating between threats and wheedling:
"Come on, Alex—I know that's your name, I heard the girl say it. Come on, man. Let's talk about this like adults. You took my people. That's not cool. We can work something out—"
"Go away, Derek," I called through the door.
"I've got a gun, dude. I will shoot through this door if you don't—"
"Titanium," Luna said, loudly enough to be heard. "Your bullets cannot go through."
Silence. Then a long string of profanity that was almost impressive in its creativity.

"I know where you live!" he screamed finally. "You think that door's going to protect you forever? I'll wait you out. I've got food, I've got people, I've got time. You're going to run out of supplies, and when you do, I'll be RIGHT HERE."
His footsteps retreated down the stairs. Not defeated. Not even deterred. Just... regrouping.

Derek Voss wasn't going to stop. He'd siege us if he had to—starve us out, wait for a mistake, exploit any crack in our defenses. He was patient in the way that only petty tyrants could be: willing to burn everything for the satisfaction of winning.

I looked at our group. Luna, the recovering zombie who could bench-press a car. Sarah, the pre-med survivor learning to trust again. Jessica, the newest arrival, still shaking. Boss, the cat, who had jumped into Jessica's lap and was purring with the determination of an animal who'd decided that this human needed him.
And me. The gamer with a magic watch and a talent for getting into trouble.
Four people and a cat, against a building full of the dead and one very alive, very angry man.
"Okay," I said, sitting down on the couch. "We need a better plan."
Sarah looked up from Jessica. "What kind of plan?"
"The kind that ends with Derek no longer being a threat. Permanently."
I said it carefully, watching their faces. Luna nodded without hesitation. Sarah's expression tightened, but she didn't object.

And Jessica, still crying, still shaking, said: "He has a baseball bat. And a gun. And he... he hurt Maria. He made her watch." Her voice cracked. "He likes it. The hurting. It's not about control. It's about the hurting."

The room went cold. Not the temperature—the atmosphere. The realization settling over all of us that Derek Voss wasn't a survivalist making hard choices. He was a predator who'd found a world without consequences.
"Then we stop him," I said. "Not because the system told us to. Not because there's a reward. Because some things need stopping."
**AFFECTION UPDATE: SARAH MITCHELL — 22%**
The number climbed. Not from romance—not yet. From something more fundamental.
From agreement.
